coredata.dc:title: A novel chemiluminescent probe based on the oxidase-like activity of pyrite quantum dots for the determination of copper in food samples
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Food Composition and Analysis
coredata.dc:creator: Seyyed Alavi, Seyyed Morteza
Amjadi, Mohammad
coredata.dc:description: Pyrite (FeS2) quantum dots (PQDs) exhibit outstanding oxidase-like activity, which can be a ground to apply them in the chemiluminescence (CL) systems. Herein, we show that PQDs in combination with luminol as an efficient CL reagent, create a powerful sensing platform. These QDs effectively catalyze the luminol oxidation with dissolved O2, producing an intense CL signal. Copper ions, having a great affinity to sulfur atoms on the surface of PQDs, adsorb onto the surface of QDs and inhibit their catalytic action, and so, diminish the CL intensity. Utilizing this effect, luminol-O2-PQDs in alkaline medium was established as a sensitive probe to determine copper in the concentration range of 2.0–100 nM with LOD of 1.2 nM. The validation of the method was performed by analysis of NIST certified reference materials, and high correlation between obtained concentration and reported values was obtained. Furthermore, the probe was applied to the detection of copper in mushroom, sunflower seeds and flour samples and comparison of the results with those obtained from a standard method approved its accuracy. Luminol-PQDs CL probe is a simple, selective, sensitive and low-cost tool for determination of copper in nutrition sources.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Quantum dots
Nanoparticles
Sensor
Food analysis

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Food Composition and Analysis

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The use of PQDs in the CL reaction
Application of PQDs-based CL for copper detection

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Step-by-step schematic representation of synthesis of iron pyrite quantum dots.
a) Fluorescence emission spectrum for PQDs excited at 360 nm and UV–Vis spectrum of × 50 diluted PQDs; b) TEM image; c) HR-TEM image; d) EDS spectrum, e) XPS spectrum, and f) XRD pattern of QDs.
a) Time profiles of the CL system in presence and absence of PQDs; Inset: time profile of CL system in the absence of QDs, and profile of QD-OH- system; b) CL spectrum of luminol-PQDs system.
Calibration curve and corresponding CL profiles for Cu2+ determination.
Inter and intra-days repeatability of the CL method for three concentrations of Cu2 + (n = 5).
Effect of interfering species on the Cu2 + (50 nM) determination.

row.entry: Fe3+, Ba2+, Albumin, Starch, Riboflavin, Glucose, Lactose, Sucrose

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Determination of copper in NIST certified samples.
Determination of copper in food samples.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: t-critical= 4.3 for 2 degrees of freedom and p = 0.05

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Comparison of the developed probe with some recent reported methods.

head.ce:title: A novel chemiluminescent probe based on the oxidase-like activity of pyrite quantum dots for the determination of copper in food samples

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Chemistry, University of Tabriz, Tabriz 5166616471, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Chemistry, University of Tabriz

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Chemistry, University of Tabriz, Tabriz 5166616471, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Pyrite (FeS2) quantum dots (PQDs) exhibit outstanding oxidase-like activity, which can be a ground to apply them in the chemiluminescence (CL) systems. Herein, we show that PQDs in combination with luminol as an efficient CL reagent, create a powerful sensing platform. These QDs effectively catalyze the luminol oxidation with dissolved O2, producing an intense CL signal. Copper ions, having a great affinity to sulfur atoms on the surface of PQDs, adsorb onto the surface of QDs and inhibit their catalytic action, and so, diminish the CL intensity. Utilizing this effect, luminol-O2-PQDs in alkaline medium was established as a sensitive probe to determine copper in the concentration range of 2.0–100 nM with LOD of 1.2 nM. The validation of the method was performed by analysis of NIST certified reference materials, and high correlation between obtained concentration and reported values was obtained. Furthermore, the probe was applied to the detection of copper in mushroom, sunflower seeds and flour samples and comparison of the results with those obtained from a standard method approved its accuracy. Luminol-PQDs CL probe is a simple, selective, sensitive and low-cost tool for determination of copper in nutrition sources.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Nowadays, application of quantum dots (QDs) in various scientific fields is enormous, but conventional QDs have some disadvantages like toxicity, complicated synthesis, and hydrophobicity that must be overcome. Earth-abundant chalcogenides are great candidates to develop green, non-toxic and biocompatible nanocrystals. A long known class of chalcogenides is pyrites and their most common member is iron pyrite that was known as “fool’s gold”. Pyrite quantum dots (PQDs) have been applied in different fields like catalysis (Yuan et al., 2016), optoelectronics (Zhai et al., 2016), solar cells (Yu et al., 2015) and energy storage devices (Douglas et al., 2015; Shao et al., 2017). They are promising nanocrystals with high potential for analytical applications. Variety and simplicity of synthesis (Guo et al., 2015a; Henríquez et al., 2020; Luo et al., 2020; Tan et al., 2015), low cost, tunable emission and band gap, low toxicity (Ahmad et al., 2018), in situ and in vitro usability are some of its desirable properties that make them eligible for detection purposes. Among all methods applied to synthesis of PQDs, non-hazardous solvent-based reflux method is the best choice because of its simplicity, controllability, high purity of the product and high yield. By synthesis under reflux, the size, shape and bandgap of QDs could be controlled by the reaction time and temperature, and iron source to sulfur source ratio, thereby nanocrystals with favorable properties can be obtained. Biocompatibility of these QDs make them suitable probe for fluorescence detection and imaging in body tissue or living micro-organisms (Wu et al., 2020; Xiao et al., 2020). Earlier, peroxidase activity of PQDs has been applied for development of colorimetric probes for detection of H2O2 and glutathione (Huang et al., 2020; Song et al., 2020). Moreover, catalytic and nanozymic properties of these nanocrystals is a potential ground to use them as chemiluminescence reaction enhancers.

Chemiluminescence (CL) is known as a simple, precise and clean method for environmental and clinical analyses. From early 30 s, methods based on emission of light during a chemical reaction have been highly regarded and developed and three decades later its theoretical principle was expounded (Albrecht, 1928; McCapra and Richardson, 1964). Among all samples analyzed with CL methods, biological and food samples are the most challenging due to their complex matrices. Preparation and pre-treatment of these samples can be difficult and time consuming, so developing methods that have a dedicated response to specific compound or element can be very advantageous and important. Simplicity of CL device, economic, minimal and portable device, broad linear range, wide range of application and high coupling capability with other methods such as separation techniques as detector or secondary analytical method brought lots of advantage with it. Along with all benefits of CL methods, limitations caused by the lack of enough CL systems compared to determinable materials have prompted researchers to use the maximum potential of CL systems with sufficient CL yield by applying various enhancers. Nanoparticles (Liu and Li, 2011; Wu and Han, 2016), QDs (Amjadi et al., 2017; Liu and Han, 2017; Zhang et al., 2018), surfactants (Guo et al., 2015b; Natrajan et al., 2011) and etc. are few examples of enhancers that are used for increasing the CL intensity of luminescent materials and obtaining a selective response.

Among all nutrition minerals and elements that is vital for enzyme systems, copper has a significant status. EAR (Estimated Average Requirement) and RDA (Recommended Dietary Allowance) of copper in adult in daily diet are 700 and 900 μg per day (Dietary Reference Intakes, 2006). The amount of copper in the daily diet can have two aspects, its deficiency can lead to anemia, osteoporosis, paleness and its higher amounts can cause health problems and copper toxicity leading to heart and kidney failure, liver damage, and brain disease (Araya et al., 2006; Harless et al., 2006; Turnlund et al., 2005). Changing copper level in body, both deficiency and over-consumption, can cause different health problems, therefor copper in daily diet and biological environment must be measured and controlled. In this work, we reported on a sensitive probe for determination of nanomolar levels of copper based on the enhanced CL system relying on PQDs oxidase-like catalytic activity.

CL intensity was monitored by Lumat LB 9507 luminometer (Berthold, Germany). Fluorescence and CL spectra were recorded by FP-8300 spectrofluorimeter (Josco, Japan), and UV-Vis spectra were recorded by Cary 100 Spectrophotometer (Varian, Australia). X-ray diffraction (XRD, TD-3700, Tongda, China), EDS, TEM (EM-280S, Philips, Netherland), HR-TEM (Tecani™ G2 F20, FEI, Japan), XPS (UHV analysis system, SPECS, Germany) were used to PQDs characterization.

All reagents used for synthesis, determination and majority of salts used in various parts of measurement were purchased from Merck (Darmstadt, Germany) with analytical grade and used without any further purification. Stock solution of Cu2+ was prepared from Cu(NO3)20.3 H2O salt. Certified reference materials (CRMs) were provided by National Institute of Standard and Technology (NIST, U.S. Department of Commerce, Gaithersburg, U.S.A.). Real samples were purchased from local supermarket. All samples and reagents were prepared with deionized water.

Blue emitting FeS2 QDs were synthesized based on inert atmosphere synthesis method according to the literature (Douglas et al., 2015; Luo et al., 2020) with some modifications. The schematic representation of the procedure is shown in Fig. 1. In a typical procedure, 1.0 mmol of iron(II) chloride was dissolved in 45 mL of degassed DMSO under nitrogen bubbling, and then 2.6 mmol of TGA was added leading to the change of color of the solution from pale yellow to blue (Fig. 1b). Solution was stirred for 30 min at room temperature and then 10 mL of Na2S2O3 solution (2.0 mmol in degassed DI water) was added to the flask dropwise. During the addition of Na2S2O3, the solution color changed from brick to burnt sienna to wine (Fig. 1c). Then the reaction set was placed in an oil bath and temperature of solution was adjusted at 120 °C and let the crystals grow for 8 h (Fig. 1d). After the solution reached to room temperature, it was centrifuged for 10 min in 5000 rpm to separate large particles, then the supernatant was collected and washed several times with ethanol and acetone to remove DMSO. Brownish participate was dissolved in DI water and used for measurements.

As a general procedure, optimum volumes of luminol (250 μL from 1 ×10−7 M solution) and QDs (100 μL from 62.5 μg.mL−1 solution) were transferred to the luminometer cuvette and suitable amount of copper standard or real sample solution was added. Final volume of the solution was set at 925 μL by adding deionized water. The cuvette is placed in the luminometer and 75 μL of 0.01 M NaOH solution was injected automatically into it. The CL intensity was monitored and maximum value was used as the analytical signal.
NIST 1643e CRM (Trace elements in water) was used directly with pH adjustment at about 6.

NIST 1566b CRM (and 1566b Oyster tissue): wet ashing method was used to prepare oyster sample. 25 mg of oyster tissue powder was transferred to a flask and 3 mL of HNO3 and 0.8 mL of perchloric acid was added to it and refluxed at 150 °C for 1 h. After that, the acidic content was dried. Pale yellow residue was dissolved in 1% HNO3 and pH was adjusted with NaOH for future use.

Mushroom sample: dry ashing method was used for preparation of mushroom sample. Mushroom was bought from local store and washed with tap and DI water, and then sliced. Five grams of sample was weighed and transferred to a watch glass and dried at 60 °C. Dry sample was transferred to a crucible and placed in an electric furnace for ashing at 500 °C for 6 h. The obtained ash was dissolved in 50 mL of 0.1 M HNO3.

Flour samples: wet ashing method was utilized for preparation of samples. Flour sample was weighed (1.0 g) and transferred to a flask and 30 mL of the concentrated HNO3 and 7 mL of perchloric acid was added to it. Sample was refluxed at 150 °C for 3 h for complete digestion. Then the obtained solution was dried at 200 °C. Residue was dissolved in 5 mL of 0.1 M HNO3 and after adjusting the solution pH at 6, the final volume was set at 50 mL.

Sunflower seeds: 5.0 g of sunflower seeds (without shell) was dried in an oven (60 °C) for 6 h and then transferred to a crucible and ashed at 500 °C for 4 h. The obtained ash was dissolved in 0.1 M HNO3 and its pH was controlled for further application.

Fluorescence spectrum of PQDs in the maximum excitation wavelength of 360 nm is shown in Fig. 2a. Maximum emission wavelength was 430 nm and the quantum yield (calculated with respect to quinine sulfate) was 12%. Absorption spectrum of PQDs was recorded (Fig. 2a), which exhibits a shoulder about 360 nm. This is similar to the spectrum reported in the literature (Douglas et al., 2015).

TEM image for estimation of PQDs size are shown in Fig. 2b. The average diameter of QDs are about 8 ± 1 nm. It should be mentioned that high content of organic solvent makes the image blur. According to HR-TEM image (Fig. 2c) the lattice distance is about 0.28 nm, which matches with the spacing of (200) crystal plane of FeS2 (Van Nguyen et al., 2019). EDS spectrum (Fig. 2d) indicates the presence of Fe and S elements.

In the XPS spectrum, Fig. 2e, two peaks at 160 eV and 220 eV correspond to sulfur’s 2p and 2 s and and a peak at 710 eV belongs to Fe 2p. The strong peak appeared about 532 eV belongs to O 1 s and may be attributed to Fe-O and O-H bonds at the surface of PQDs caused by surface oxidation of iron sulfide (creating FexOy and FeOOH) and adsorption of water onto the surface (Chanturiya et al., 2011; Chanturiya et al., 2019). X-ray diffraction pattern, Fig. 2f, shows six main peaks at 2θ= 28.5, 33.1, 37.1, 43.5,45.8, 56.8 belong to (111), (200), (210), (211), (220), (311) and three weak peaks at 2θ= 59.0, 61.7, 64.3 refer to (222), (230), (321), which according to ICDD ref. No. 01–073–8127 and RRUFF R100166 confirm successful synthesis of PQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The use of PQDs in the CL reaction
ce:section.ce:para:
Luminol in the alkaline solution exhibits a weak CL, which arises from its oxidation reaction with dissolved O2. When FeS2 QDs are added to the solution, the CL intensity is remarkably enhanced ( Fig. 3a). The enhancing effect of PQDs is attributed to their oxidize-like activity. The oxidize-like activity of QDs was proved by testing with TMB; while blank solution remained almost colorless, the solution containing QDs quickly turned to blue and the absorption peak appeared in the region of 650 nm (Fig S1, Electronic Supplementary Material (ESM)). The raw materials effect on the CL system was also investigated. Fe2+ had a very small enhancement effect on the CL reaction so that the catalytic effect of QDs could not be due to this cation. Deoxygenation of luminol-PQDs solution by bubbling N2 caused a decrease in the CL intensity to the background level, which indicates the vital role of dissolved oxygen in the reaction. As previously reported, impeller engine of luminol-oxygen CL system is reactive oxygen species (ROS), which are highly active oxidizers such as hydroxyl (•OH) and superoxide (O2 •-) radicals. Electronically excited 3-aminophthalate anion that is the product of luminol oxidization with ROS is known as the emitting species in the luminol-O2 system. The CL spectrum of luminol-O2-PQDs system was recorded using fluorimeter and shown in Fig. 3b. The emission peak at 425 nm indicates that the emitting species in this system is 3-aminophthalate, too. The influence of different radical scavengers was also tested on the CL system. As can be seen in Table S1 (ESM) deoxygenation and adding ascorbic acid almost completely quench the CL intensity. Also, the elimination of •OH and
O
2
• −
(by thiourea and glutathione) caused significant reduction in the emission intensity (Apak et al., 2022). All evidence suggests that PQDs are most likely act as catalysts and accelerate the generation of ROS, thereby increasing the CL intensity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of PQDs-based CL for copper detection
ce:section.ce:para: Potential application of PQDs-enhanced CL system as an analytical tool was studied in the next step. At first, the CL system was optimized to achieve highest sensitivity. 25 nM of luminol, 0.75 mM of NaOH and 6.25 μg/mL of PQDs were chosen as the optimum values (Fig S2, ESM). In these conditions, the effect of several cations on the CL system was tested and found that Cu2+ had a highest quenching effect on the system.

The effect of copper on the CL system can be attributed to the tendency of Cu2+ to be coordinated with the surface sulfur groups of the PQDs. It is known that Cu(II) species can adsorb on the pyrite surface converting it to a copper sulfide-like surface (Leppinen, 1990; Guo et al., 2017). The most likely form of the copper sulfide formed at the surface of pyrite is believed to be Cu2S. We suspect that the formation of such layer at the surface of PQDs diminishes its catalytic activity. As a result, the concentration of ROS and thus CL intensity are significantly reduced. This effect was used as an analytical platform for Cu2+ detection.

In the next step, the analytical performance of PQDs-enhanced system as Cu2+ probe was assessed. Calibration curve was obtained under optimal conditions and shown in Fig. 4. The probe exhibited a log-log linear response to Cu2+ in the range of 2.0–100.0 nM, with the equation logΔI= 0.165logC+ 4.445, R2 = 0.998, in which ΔI is the signal difference in the absence and presence of Cu(II) and C is the concentration in nM. The detection limit defined as 3 sy/x/b (where sy/x is the standard error of the regression, which is an estimate of the random error in y-direction, and b is slope of the calibration curve (Miller and Miller, 2010) was 1.2 nM. The intra-day and inter-day repeatability of the assay in terms of standard deviation (RSD %) were given in Table 1.

The selectivity of the method was studied against the interference of some major species found in real samples. The results are summarized in Table 2. Except Cr(VI), other ions have no significant effect on the determination of copper. Concentration of Cr(VI) in various food samples is usually much lower than Cu2+ and so it can be said that this CL probe is sufficiently selective for Cu2+. The good selectivity of the assay is probably due to the specific interaction of Cu2+ with pyrite, which discussed above.

In the last step, PQDs-based probe was applied to determine copper in several real samples. Firstly, the accuracy of the probe was studied by measuring copper content of two certified reference materials (NIST 1643e Trace element in water and 1566b Oyster tissue). Results shown in the Table 3 confirm the accuracy of the method. Then, several real food samples were analyzed by the method and the results are given in Table 4. The recoveries showed the closeness of the obtained concentrations with the added amounts and the t-test showed the proper correlation of the obtained values. Also, comparing the results with the values obtained from the atomic absorption spectrometry (AAS) confirmed the accuracy of the method.

A brief comparison of the developed probe with some similar optical probes ( Table 5) showed the acceptable performance of PQDs-based CL method for detection of trace amounts of copper without applying extraction techniques. Small amounts of chemicals consumed, easy preparation, and speed and ease of implementation are advantages of this CL probe.

Pyrite quantum dots were synthesized by a simple solvothermal method and its oxidase-like activity was utilized to develop a CL probe for detection of trace amount of Cu2+. Copper ion quenches luminol-O2-PQDs CL system which can be employed as analytical platform for detection of copper content of food samples such as flour, sunflower and mushroom. Simplicity and low cost of synthesis procedure and low amount and green chemicals, simple device and method’s selectivity and sensitivity make the developed probe a sustainable tool for detection of copper in complex matrices.
Seyed Morteza Seyed Alavi: Investigation, Validation, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Mohammad Amjadi: Conceptualization, Supervision, Resources, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jfca.2023.105402.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:

.
